Title: Man found dead inside car had past record of 20 criminal offences
Article:
BALING: Police have confirmed that the body found inside a car by the roadside here yesterday was that of a local man with a prior record involving 20 general and drug-related cases.
District police chief Superintendent Brandon Richard Joe also said checks revealed that the car had been reported stolen in Pendang.
He said police received a report at about 11.35am regarding an individual found inside a car at Simpang 3 Tawar.
Upon arriving at the scene, police found the car locked, and the man inside was confirmed dead.
"A team from the Baling Fire and Rescue Station assisted in unlocking the vehicle and extricating the body.
"The Kedah Contingent Police Headquarters Forensic Unit also conducted investigations at the scene," he said.
Initial investigations showed that the deceased was a man in his 30s. Further checks revealed that he had a past record involving 20 general crime and narcotics cases.
'Our checks also revealed that the Proton Wira used by the man had been reported stolen in Pendang. That case is being investigated under Section 379A of the Penal Code for vehicle theft.
"The vehicle has been seized for further investigation," he added.
The body was sent to the Sultanah Bahiyah Hospital Forensic Department in Alor Setar for a post-mortem.
The case has been classified as sudden death.
It was previously reported that firefighters were called in to force open a locked car at after a body was discovered inside the vehicle. The victim was found seated in the front passenger seat of the car.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US, Mexico border agencies coordinate ahead of World Cup
Article:
SAN DIEGO: United States and Mexican border authorities are working together to prepare for increased cross-border movement during the 2026 Fifa World Cup.
US Customs and Border Protection (CBP)  Director of Field Operations for San Diego, Sidney Aki, said the agency was already working with the Mexican government to manage movement through ports of entry.
The tournament will be jointly hosted by the US, Mexico and Canada, making cross-border travel a key part of tournament logistics for some teams, officials and football fans.
Aki said San Diego was expected to see some of that movement, especially as travellers move between the US and Mexico for matches or related activities.
Aki said both countries understood that border security was a shared responsibility, especially with the World Cup placing added attention on travel between the two countries.
[[nid:1446854]]
"Obviously, this is a legal port of entry. A lot of the movement, with regard to cross-border travel, will be utilised through here in San Diego.
"We understand our responsibility. This is a shared border. This is both our countries' border, and that is a huge responsibility for both countries to ensure that we are doing the best to keep people safe," he said during a briefing with foreign journalists in San Ysidro.
He said once individuals linked to the tournament, including players or officials, were identified, information would be shared with CBP to allow checks to be conducted before they entered the US.
"That information will then be fed to us. We will conduct our background checks, and then, of course, assist with efficient processing of these individuals entering the US, as well as them going to Mexico," he said.
Aki said while he did not have an estimate on the expected increase in travellers, CBP was prepared to manage higher volumes because San Diego ports of entry already handled large numbers of people daily.
He said CBP relied on several layers of screening, including X-ray systems, canine units, officer interviews and border technology, to keep crossings secure while allowing lawful movement to continue.
"The more secure we are, the more emphasis we place on security, the more efficient we are with regard to lawful trade and lawful travel into the US," he added.
The San Diego-Tijuana border region is home to the San Ysidro Port of Entry, which is one of the busiest land border crossing in the world.
Note: The writer is in the United States for a reporting tour on the country's customs and border security system, organised by the US Department of State's Foreign Press Centre. The briefing in San Diego was part of the programme.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Heartwarming rescue of monkey on rain-lashed highway goes viral [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A touching video documenting the rescue of a dusky leaf monkey found stranded in the middle of a busy highway in Kulai, Johor during a heavy downpour has gone viral.
The story gained traction after veterinarian Dr Marlia Zulkapli shared the journey of the monkey, which was discovered on the road in torrential rain.
Despite reports that the monkey had actually been run over, it appeared to be in good shape and showed no signs of serious injury following the incident.
One of the most moving aspects of the rescue was the unexpected cooperation shown by other motorists on the expressway.
Dr Marlia said that the traffic seemed to part naturally to allow the rescue to take place safely.
"The best part was how all the cars seemed to understand the situation. Suddenly the road cleared, and there wasn't much noise. It was as if God had decreed that everyone must give way today.
"It seems its life was meant to be longer. We were just lucky to be the ones who could provide the path to safety" She said.
Despite being a wild animal, the monkey remained calm throughout the ordeal.
Footage shared by the vet showed the monkey sitting quietly on a passenger's lap during the car ride, appearing to trust its rescuers.

Later updates showed the monkey safely housed in a cage, looking alert and contentedly snacking on a piece of fruit.
The post has since been flooded with comments from netizens who were moved by both the rescue and the rare display of patience from Malaysian drivers.
"Faith in humanity restored. It's not often you see everyone on a highway agree to slow down and give way for a small life," commented a user.
"The way the monkey just sat on the lap, it knew it was in safe hands. Thank you to the kind souls who stopped to help in such bad weather," A netizen said.
The video continues to circulate across social media platforms, serving as an inspiration to many.

Title: No restrictions yet on non-Islamic houses of worship in Selangor commercial areas
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Selangor government has not enforced or implemented newly drawn-up planning guidelines that bar non-Islamic houses of worship from being built in commercial areas.
State Special Committee for Non-Islamic Affairs (Limas) co-chairman Ng Sze Han said the state government will carry out a review of the guidelines in consultation with religious associations, with a meeting on the issue to take place early next month.
Ng was referring to the recently published Selangor State Planning Guidelines and Standards for Community Facilities, which are administered by the state town and country planning department, PLANMalaysia Selangor.
"The state government wishes to address recent concerns regarding PLANMalaysia Selangor's guidelines on non-Muslim places of worship, specifically concerning the approval of future applications for non-Muslim places of worship in commercial shoplots.
"As such, we wish to clarify that the guidelines have not yet been implemented or enforced by any local authority," Ng, who is also Selangor investment, trade and mobility chairman, said in a statement.
He also stressed that no enforcement action will take place while the review of the guidelines is ongoing.
"The state government is dedicated to resolving this administrative and technical alignment through constructive dialogue, ensuring that Selangor remains a progressive, fair, and inclusive state for all."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Barrier-free toll goal could hit speed bump, warn experts
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  Widespread toll evasion could undermine Malaysia's ambitions to modernise its highway network through the Multi-Lane Free Flow (MLFF) toll collection system, experts said.
They said  weak enforcement and poor motorist compliance may threaten the success of the system.
Universiti Teknologi Mara head of postgraduate studies at the Malaysian Institute of Transport, Dr Abdul Khabir Rahmat, said toll evasion must be addressed seriously.
"If non-compliance still occurs under the  toll system, which has physical barriers and payment controls, enforcement challenges under MLFF will become even more complex."
It was reported that about 95 per cent of toll evasion cases on the Kuala Lumpur-Karak Expressway and the East Coast Expressway since 2023 involve Class One vehicles, which are private vehicles with two axles and three or four wheels, such as sedans, hatchbacks, and SUVs.
AFA Prime Bhd general manager Ismaliza Ibrahim said 2,122 letters of demand  were issued between 2023 and 2025, amounting to RM21,181 for  offences at toll plazas.
She said toll evasion offences continued despite  enforcement and monitoring measures.
Khabir said MLFF was a modern toll collection approach that allowed vehicles to pass through highways without stopping at toll plazas, relying on technologies such as Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR), electronic identification systems and real-time digital payment processing.
"International experience showed MLFF could  reduce congestion, improve traffic flow, lower carbon emissions and increase highway capacity without requiring major infrastructure expansion.
"However,  implementation depended not only on technology, but also on robust digital enforcement, integrated data systems and effective payment recovery mechanisms.
"MLFF is not merely about removing toll barriers, but about transforming the  enforcement philosophy from physical control to digital compliance."
Meanwhile, Professor Dr Munzilah Md Rohani of Universiti Tun Hussein Onn Malaysia's Faculty of Civil Engineering and Built Environment said toll evasion  should be viewed as a behavioural issue rather than a technological failure.
"Even under the  gated toll system, some motorists still manage to evade payment.
"Under MLFF, every transaction and vehicle movement will be digitally recorded, so failure to pay should be treated as an intentional offence, not a system weakness."
Munzilah said one of the biggest risks of widespread toll evasion would be revenue leakages, which could affect concessionaires' ability to maintain highways, uphold safety standards and fund future infrastructure upgrades.
She   said  enforcement agencies could become overwhelmed if unpaid toll cases surged, especially if penalties remained too lenient to act as an effective deterrent.
"Malaysia often gives too much discretion to offenders, making it difficult to cultivate discipline among motorists.
"Enforcement may need to become stricter and more decisive."
Munzilah said Malaysia could emulate countries such as Australia and Taiwan as examples, that combine ANPR technology, automatic payment deductions and strict penalties for non-payment.
She said Malaysia should adopt a dual-layer enforcement model combining RFID (Radio Frequency Identification) and ANPR, integrated with the Road Transport Department's database, to strengthen compliance and reduce fraud.
Beyond enforcement, she said flexible payment options, including direct debit, e-wallet integration, mobile apps and incentive programmes, could encourage greater public compliance.
Munzilah stressed  the need for safeguards to protect motorists from technical glitches, including grace periods, payment notifications and accessible dispute resolution mechanisms.
"MLFF should not only be seen as a technological upgrade for highways, but as part of a broader transformation towards a more modern and responsible driving culture in Malaysia."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Perak PKR wants Anwar to stay in Tambun for GE16
Article:
IPOH: Perak PKR wants Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim to remain in Tambun for the next general election to strengthen the party's election campaign in the state.
Its chairman, Mohamad Hairul Amir Sabri said the presence of the prime minister and PKR president was crucial in strengthening the party's position in Perak.
"We in Perak certainly need the president to remain here to help us win more parliamentary and state seats in the next election," he told reporters after the opening of the new Perak PKR headquarters in Meru here yesterday.
Also present was Perak PKR deputy chairman Tan Kar Hing.
He was responding to speculation over where Anwar would contest in the next general election, with some suggesting the Tambun member of parliament could be fielded in Penang or Putrajaya.
Hairul said Anwar's continued presence in Perak would be key to strengthening the party machinery and improving Pakatan Harapan's (PH) performance in the state, particularly in securing more parliamentary and state seats.
"Actually, it is still too early for us to know.
"Based on our experience with Datuk Seri (Anwar), he usually makes decisions only two or three months before the election.
"For him, it is not about whether a seat is safe or not. For example, when he came to Tambun recently, the purpose was to build momentum and support in the northern region so that we can win more seats in Perak and contribute more seats at the federal level.
"At the moment, I do not think there will be any changes. We in Perak certainly need the president to remain here to help us win more parliamentary and state seats in the next election," he said.
In the 15th General Election, Anwar won the Tambun parliamentary seat with a majority of 3,736 votes.
He secured 49,625 votes, defeating Datuk Seri Ahmad Faizal Azumu of Perikatan Nasional, who garnered 45,889 votes.
Barisan Nasional candidate Datuk Aminuddin Md Hanafiah received 28,140 votes, while Parti Pejuang Tanah Air candidate Abdul Rahim Tahir obtained 1,115 votes.
On speculation of internal unrest following recent resignations involving the PKR Tanjung Malim branch Youth chief, he downplayed the matter, saying it had not affected the party's overall structure in the state.
"In Tanjung Malim, out of the committee lineup, only a small number stepped down. The majority remain and the branch continues its work as usual," he said.
On Wednesday, PKR Tanjung Malim Youth chief Muhammad Haziq Azfar Ishak announced his resignation as a PKR Youth exco member and his immediate departure from PKR.
Haziq said the decision was made due to concerns over the party's direction, which he claimed had increasingly deviated from its original struggle and principles.
Hairul also dismissed suggestions of a significant exodus of members in Perak, including claims linking them to a newly formed political platform.
"For now, there is no involvement of senior leadership or division chiefs. The numbers involved are very small compared with our overall membership of more than 100,000 in Perak," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: HR Ministry highlights reforms, benefits and protections for migrant workers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Human Resources Ministry is refining reforms to address the root causes of mental health issues among migrant workers, particularly labour rights abuses and financial pressures.
The ministry said there is a direct link between mental health and labour exploitation, citing the International Labour Organisation's (ILO) recognition of the "abuse of vulnerability" as a key indicator of forced labour.
Acknowledging that mental health issues among migrant workers are severely underreported, it said figures recorded by the Occupational Safety and Health Department do not reflect reality.
"The low numbers are due to severe underreporting, lack of awareness strong cultural stigma surrounding mental health," the ministry said, adding that language barriers and fear of deportation also contribute.
Human rights advocates have said underlying pressures, including poor working and living conditions such as long hours, recruitment debt, cramped housing, fear of termination, and limited grievance channels, continue to affect migrant workers.
The ministry said migrant workers are generally in good physical and mental condition upon arrival based on mandatory screenings, but acknowledged these assessments focus largely on physical health rather than psychosocial well-being. To address this gap, the National Institute of Occupational Safety and Health is promoting Employee Assistance Programmes to enable early detection of mental health issues.
This is complemented by the extension of Social Security Organisation (Perkeso) benefits to foreign workers under the Employees' Social Security Act.
Mental health conditions arising from workplace accidents, including post-traumatic stress disorder, are covered under Perkeso's Employment Injury Scheme, while its Invalidity Scheme also includes conditions that render a worker unable to earn a living.
The ministry also highlighted amendments to the Employment Act in 2023, which reduced the maximum work week to 45 hours and strengthened rest day enforcement.
The Employees' Minimum Standards of Housing, Accommodations and Amenities Act was expanded to all sectors in 2021 and extended to Sabah and Sarawak in May 2025, standardising housing rights nationwide.
Workers can also use the e-Aduan mobile application to lodge complaints on wage theft, abuse, or passport confiscation directly with the Labour Department.
The app supports several languages, including Bahasa Melayu, English, Nepali and Bengali.
The ministry added that migrant workers are now allowed to change employers across industries, a policy introduced in May last year to break the "single-employer visa trap" that prevents abused workers from leaving.
"In severe cases, workers whose complaints meet the threshold for forced labour or human trafficking are protected under the Anti-Trafficking in Persons and Anti-Smuggling of Migrants framework.
"This grants them Interim Protection Orders, shelter, immigration immunity and the right to work legally while their cases are heard," it said.
On international commitments, the ministry said Malaysia ratified the ILO Protocol to the Forced Labour Convention in 2022, binding the country to prevent forced labour and protect victims.
"In consultation with the ILO and civil society groups, we are drafting the National Action Plan on Forced Labour, focusing on stronger grievance mechanisms and zero-cost migration commitments.
"When a worker suffers from poor mental health, they are far less likely to report abuses, advocate for themselves, or leave an exploitative situation," the ministry said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Another Malaysian haj pilgrim dies in Holy Land, bringing total to nine
Article:
MAKKAH: A 77‑year‑old Malaysian male haj pilgrim died here on Thursday due to intra‑abdominal sepsis.
In a statement, Lembaga Tabung Haji (TH) said the pilgrim, from Machang, Kelantan, was pronounced dead at King Abdul Aziz Hospital at 5.40pm.
According to TH, he had travelled to the Holy Land with his wife and was part of the KT50 group, staying at the Abraj Al‑Tayseer Hotel.
"His remains were brought to Masjidil Haram for funeral prayers after Subuh yesterday before being laid to rest at Al Sharayea Cemetery in Makkah," it said.
TH also advised haj pilgrims to take care of their health, get sufficient rest and seek immediate treatment at TH healthcare centres if they experience any health problems while in the Holy Land.
The death of the pilgrim from Machang brings the total number of Malaysian haj pilgrims who have died in the Holy Land for the 1447H/2026M haj season to nine so far.– BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Debt, abuse and isolation push domestic workers to the brink
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: For domestic workers in Malaysia, the mental health crisis often begins not at the workplace, but at the recruiter's door — with fees that leave them in debt before they even start work.
Suherman, a migrant worker and founding member of Serantau Malaysia, an organisation advocating for the welfare of Indonesian migrants, said domestic workers, most of whom are women, face intense pressure.
"The recruitment costs for domestic workers are extremely high, reaching up to RM15,000.
"After paying such high recruitment fees, the pressure at the workplace can be harsh," he said.
Suherman said many distressed migrant workers share the same experience — being denied basic rights.
"We are supposed to be given one day off a week, but this does not happen.
"We are supposed to be given communication devices to contact family or friends, but this is also not provided," he said.
Wage withholding, an illegal practice, is also common, he added.
Suherman acknowledged that while government reforms have reduced such abuses, the practices persist.
"Workers are pressured, wages are withheld and they think about their children back home who need schooling and other necessities.
"That is where mental anguish begins."
Up to June 30, 2019, the Home Ministry recorded 129,168 registered domestic workers, of whom 72 per cent were from Indonesia (92,405) and 25 per cent from the Philippines (32,277), with the remainder from other South and Southeast Asian countries.
The International Organisation for Migration has highlighted that domestic workers are excluded from key provisions of the Employment Act, including minimum rest days, regulated working hours, annual and sick leave, overtime compensation and maternity benefits.
Among migrant workers, Suherman said domestic workers are the most vulnerable.
"Those who most commonly face mental health problems are domestic workers," he said, adding that unlike labourers, they work alone and lack peer support.
He said emotional abuse by employers compounds the harm, as domestic workers are labelled as carriers of disease and isolated from the rest of the household.
Suherman said cost and awareness remain barriers to accessing mental health support.
Physical health coverage provided by employers, he added, is often minimal, forcing workers to pay out of pocket. There is no coverage for mental health.
At the heart of the crisis are dignified working conditions.
"Working conditions should not be overly oppressive and stigma must be reduced. If employers provide a sense of comfort, even if the work is heavy, there will be no problem," he said.
Meanwhile, Indonesia-based migrant NGO MigrantCare's Malaysia representative Alex Ong said his organisation has handled cases involving detention, deportation and family separation.
"We had a cleaner who complained about wage theft, was rescued but ejected from her hostel and placed in a detention centre," Ong said.
"She was treated like a criminal, chained and handcuffed when deported."
Others, he said, were forced into domestic work despite holding cleaner permits.
"Mental health support is hardly available to migrants," he said, adding that NGOs are largely limited to basic counselling on legal or livelihood issues.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bomb threat at prominent KL building a hoax, police confirm
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have confirmed that a bomb threat made against a prominent building in the capital last night was false.
Brickfields district police chief ACP Hoo Chang Hook said police received a call at 9.09pm from a security guard at the building.
"The building's security guard informed us that he had received an email sent to the customer service centre stating that an individual had placed a bomb on the 23rd floor of the building's north tower," he said in a statement.
According to him, a Bomb Disposal Unit, K9 Unit and Forensic Unit were deployed to the location to conduct a search, which confirmed the threat was false.
The case is being investigated under Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 and Section 507 of the Penal Code.– BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mental health expert calls for systemic reforms for migrant workers' well-being
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: An expert has called for culturally tailored helplines, protections against job loss or deportation for seeking help and mental health training to address a growing crisis among migrant workers.
Malaysian Mental Health Association (MMHA) president Professor Datuk Dr Andrew Mohanraj also called for mandatory employer responsibilities ­— safe accommodation, reasonable working hours, guaranteed leave and access to medical care.
He said these measures should be supported by routine death audits, transparent data sharing across agencies and stronger community mental health capacity through NGO-government partnerships.
Commenting on mental health issues affecting foreigners, particularly those from Nepal, Andrew said financial pressure, loneliness and exhaustion were key drivers of poor mental health and suicide.
It was previously reported by the Nepal embassy that 1,158 Nepali migrant workers in Malaysia had died from 2018 to July 2021, with suicide among the key causes.
"Loneliness is a well-documented risk factor for depression and suicide," he said.
Complicating matters for Nepali workers, he said, are cultural barriers that limit help-seeking.
"In Nepal, there is a strong stigma around mental illness and suicide. People may interpret symptoms as moral weakness rather than a treatable illness."
Many male migrants, he added, fear that admitting distress means failing in their role as family providers.
He also highlighted how Western mental health terminology may not align with Nepali expressions of distress, making it harder for workers to communicate their condition and for clinicians to identify it.
The physical environment compounds the harm. Andrew said overcrowded and poorly maintained dormitories increase stress and hinder recovery, with poor sleep, lack of privacy and the spread of disease worsening mood disorders and anxiety.
Persistent night shifts, long hours and lack of leave contribute to burnout, while workplace injuries and unsafe conditions add layers of trauma and economic insecurity.
Andrew said mental health services remain largely inaccessible to migrant workers due to legal, financial and policy barriers.
Culturally appropriate services are even scarcer. While NGOs and international agencies fill critical gaps, they remain severely under-resourced.
"Government clinics can provide basic care if language support is available, but many clinicians lack training in cross-cultural assessment and in detecting suicide risk among migrant workers," he said.
Andrew warned that severe depression and anxiety can lead to hopelessness, impaired decision-making and weakened problem-solving, while substance use and sleep deprivation increase impulsivity and vulnerability to accidents or self-harm.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Punishment for toll dodgers: Fines can deter offenders, jail may be too harsh [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: While criminalising toll evasion could be one way to deter offenders, Works Minister Datuk Seri Alexander Nanta Linggi said the government must strike a balance between effective punishment and fairness.
Nanta said although fines could serve as a deterrent, some motorists had become accustomed to paying penalties without changing their behaviour.
He said the government is working to resolve enforcement concerns ahead of the Multi-Lane Free Flow (MLFF) toll collection rollout.
"Fines are one thing, but sometimes people get used to paying fines and think it is no big deal.
"But if we go as far as imposing jail sentences, that may be too much as well.
"So there must be a balance in terms of punishment for those who wrongly use our roads," he told the
New Straits Times
.
[[nid:1446831]]
He was responding to questions on whether the government was considering criminalising toll evasion, following reports of repeat offenders running through toll plazas, including cases involving motorists committing dozens of offences.
Nanta said toll evasion was not a new issue and was among the key concerns being addressed as part of discussions on MLFF implementation.
"Many countries have implemented the MLFF concept, so this is not something that cannot be resolved.
"But the issue is how much leakage can be tolerated, where people pass through without paying and are not identified."
He said this was among the reasons negotiations with concessionaires had taken longer, as stakeholders wanted assurance that revenue losses from payment evasion would remain negligible.
"It is tedious and complicated because all players want to be sure they do not suffer losses.
"Even if the losses are small, it is still money."
Nanta said discussions were also ongoing over enforcement jurisdiction, including which authority would be responsible for acting against offenders and what measures would be taken against those caught evading payment.
He added that safeguards must also be in place to protect motorists from being wrongly flagged as toll evaders.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Large police presence around White House after reports of shots fired
Article:
WASHINGTON: Police and security forces swarmed the White House on Saturday evening after shots were fired in the area, authorities said.
US President Donald Trump was at the White House at the time as he worked to negotiate a deal with Iran.
Police cordoned off access to the White House and National Guard troops blocked an AFP reporter from entering the area in downtown Washington.
"FBI is on the scene and supporting Secret Service responding to shots fired near White House grounds," FBI Director Kash Patel said on X.
Canadian tourist Reid Adrian told AFP he was in the area when "we heard probably 20 to 25 what sounded like fireworks, but they're gunshots, and then everyone started running."
Journalists who were on the White House North Lawn at the time said on X that they were ordered to run and shelter in the press briefing room.
ABC News correspondent Selina Wang had been recording a video for social media when the apparent gunfire broke out, capturing the sounds of the shots as she dove to the ground.
"It sounded like dozens of gunshots," she said on X.
A Secret Service spokesman told AFP in a text message that the agency was still gathering information about the incident.
Trump, 79, has been the target of three alleged assassination attempts, the most recent of which took place on April 25 when an armed man stormed a security checkpoint near the ballroom where Trump was attending a media gala.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Isolation, mounting debts add to mental health issues among migrant workers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Chandra Mogar, 42, gives little away.
Seated at a Nepali restaurant tucked away in the city centre, the small-built security guard answers each question politely and briefly.
"I was happy. No issues," he said, as if the mental health episode that left him hospitalised had never happened.
Purna, a representative from the non-profit Pravasi Nepali Coordination Committee (PNCC) Malaysia, fills the silences, painting a fuller picture — one that Chandra himself seems reluctant to share.
"What I have observed from him is that he would work at one place for three months, then when he does not like it anymore, he would move to another place," Purna said, adding that Chandra has repeated this pattern for the past four years.
Yet in the same breath, Chandra said he was happy. Purna gave a small shrug.
The "episode", which occurred in January, began when Chandra woke up dizzy with a headache and vomited through the night.
He went to hospital, was warded for a day, and remembers nothing of the three days that followed.
"After four days, I came to my senses. I do not know how," he said in an interview in January.
Purna said during those three days, Chandra called him repeatedly, ranting about missing money that was actually in his own bag. He knocked on dormitory doors at odd hours and asked, in daylight, to be taken into the forest at night.
"He would say many things," Purna said.
Chandra listened with a faint, awkward smile.
Purna believes the episode was caused by alcohol withdrawal.
Chandra arrived in Malaysia eight years ago, leaving his wife and son in Nepal. He worked 12-hour shifts and lived in a dormitory where his social world extended to three or four friends.
He drank at least 350ml of alcohol every night, the equivalent of a small water bottle, after every shift. He had drunk in Nepal too, but drank more here.
Purna drew a distinction between what he called the "originals", branded bottles sold in licensed stores for around RM90, and the contraband that circulates among migrant worker communities at RM35 to RM40 a bottle.
The duplicates are mixed by hand and sold illegally. A bottle might be labelled 48 per cent alcohol. The actual content, Purna said, is anyone's guess.
Alcohol, Purna said, is only one of three pathways that lead migrant workers into mental health crises.
The second is mobile gambling, where workers lose an entire month's wages in a single night and face the morning unable to pay debts or send money home.
The third is stress from workplaces that demand more than a person can give, and families back home that need more than a person can send.
Chandra had the third as well, though it took a colleague of Purna's to say it aloud.
His son had been calling him for money. His wife does not work. Chandra had stopped picking up the calls.
When it was mentioned, Chandra fell silent and looked at the table. The poker face, for a moment, slipped.
"He does have family issues," Purna said.
It took Purna, the Nepal embassy and several weeks of administrative work to get Chandra, who was undocumented, repatriated under the government's Migrant Repatriation Programme.
He cannot return to Malaysia.
A week after the interview, he boarded a plane home to a country where there is no work, to a wife and a son whose calls he had stopped answering.
"I miss them," he had said. It was, in the end, the most genuine thing he said.
CRUSHED BY STRESS
In a shoplot on the other side of the city, a young, frail man sobs softly by his bunk bed, barely mustering a response when Purna greets him.
Pais, 22, came to Malaysia to work, with the unglamorous ambition of sending money home.
A month after arriving, he fell ill. His company took him to a clinic, then a hospital. The diagnosis was syphilis — treatable, curable with time and the right medication.
But his fellow workers in the dormitory had repeatedly told him that what he had could not be cured, that he was going to die. He believed them.
"When you're already stressed by your circumstances, and colleagues tell you that you are going to die from an incurable disease, his mind becomes distorted," Purna said.
Pais could not eat. He had grown visibly thinner. His head spun constantly. He could not work, which meant he could not earn, which meant the debt he had taken on to come to Malaysia kept growing with nothing to offset it.
He waited weeks for the infection to clear before he could return home.
The waiting, in a dormitory where the men who had told him he was dying still lived and worked, was its own cruelty.
Purna sat with him for nearly half an hour, moving between reassurance and crude jokes that drew a faint smile, then another. By the end, the sobbing had stopped.
"When I met him, he was crying uncontrollably. What you see now is a lot of progress," Purna said.
What remained was a frightened young man waiting to go home, having earned nothing. But he was no longer sobbing. He was, just barely, present.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kyiv hit by massive ballistic missile barrage
Article:
KYIV: A "mass ballistic missile attack" pounded Kyiv early Sunday, military authorities said, with AFP journalists hearing heavy explosions, after Russia threatened retaliation for Ukrainian strikes in the east.
A residential building located close to the government district shook from the explosions and dozens of people took shelter in an underground metro station in central Kyiv, AFP reporters saw, as officials urged people to take cover.
"The capital has come under a mass ballistic missile attack," Tymur Tkachenko, head of the Kyiv City Military Administration, wrote on Telegram.
"There are currently reports of at least 4 locations affected by the attack: Shevchenkivsky, Dniprovsky and Podilsky districts. Fires and damage to residential buildings are preliminarily reported," he added.
"A strike drone attack is ongoing; the ballistic missile threat remains present. Stay in shelters!"
Kyiv's Mayor Vitali Klitschko said medical teams were called to the Podilsky district in the northwest where debris fell in a non-residential area, and a fire started near a residential building in the nearby Shevchenkivsky district, with windows damaged.
On Saturday Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy warned of the risk of a major Russian airstrike in the coming hours with weapons including the Oreshnik, a Russian nuclear-capable missile.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Breaking point: The mental health crisis facing migrant workers [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: In six years, 344 Nepali migrant workers have taken their own lives, raising serious concerns over foreign workers' mental health.
Alarm bells were first raised in 2021 when the Human Rights Commission of Malaysia (Suhakam) revealed suicide as a leading cause of death among Nepali migrants.
Nearly five years on, human rights advocates say the underlying pressures, including working and living conditions, persist and may be worsening.
Pusat Komas director and former Suhakam commissioner Jerald Joseph, who first highlighted the findings, said input from the Nepali embassy revealed pressures and tensions that drove workers to take their own lives.
This led to Suhakam signing a memorandum of understanding with its Nepali counterpart, allowing returning workers to lodge complaints about their time in Malaysia.
Workers cited long hours, debt from recruitment fees, cramped and unsanitary living conditions, fear of termination leading to undocumented status and a lack of proper complaint channels as key issues.
Under the Employment Act, regular working hours cannot exceed eight hours a day. Total working hours, including overtime, are capped at 12 hours a day ­— the standard shift length for security guards.
"All this adds up. It would not be surprising if the stress and pressure push some to take their own lives," he said.
For context, the 344 Nepali workers who took their own lives over a six-year period were among the 2,214 workers from the country who died here.
[[nid:1446801]]
While the spotlight in 2021 was on Nepali workers, Jerald said such pressures affected migrant communities across the board.
"A new Bangladeshi worker from a village may be shocked by how different Malaysia is. Indonesian workers may cope better due to language familiarity, but this is not unique to Nepali workers.
"Anyone living abroad for long periods without a support system can either grow stronger or break under the strain," he said.
He added that migrant workers also face xenophobia.
"The migrant community is often looked down upon. Just look at the comments online."
On mental health support, Jerald said the situation was dire, with services for migrants still limited.
Suhakam said Malaysia's healthcare system remains largely inaccessible to migrants facing job loss and psychological distress.
Barriers such as cost, language differences and fear of immigration consequences prevent many in crisis from seeking help.
"Availability must be matched with accessibility, particularly for vulnerable and marginalised groups," Suhakam said.
The commission added that mental health is not adequately integrated into labour protections, and the Covid-19 pandemic exposed major gaps in existing frameworks.
Suhakam said Malaysia could adopt international best practices, including integrating mental health into labour and occupational safety frameworks, providing multilingual helplines and expanding community-based outreach programmes.
It also proposed requiring employers to ensure access to counselling, strengthening the regulation of recruitment practices to reduce debt-related stress and establishing accessible grievance mechanisms that workers can use without fear of retaliation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: High Court awards Nigerian RM675,000 over unlawful detention: A-GC mulls appeal
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Attorney-General's Chambers (A-GC) is reviewing the High Court's decision which awarded a Nigerian man RM675,000 in damages after finding that he was unlawfully detained for about 40 days in 2021.
Attorney-General Tan Sri Mohd Dusuki Mokhtar said the matter is currently being studied.
"We are studying the matter and will make a decision as soon as possible. All angles will be taken into consideration," he told the New Straits Times.
The High Court on Friday awarded a Nigerian man RM675,000 in damages after ruling that he
was unlawfully detained for about 40 days
by the authorities in 2021.
Judge Jamhirah Ali allowed Simon Adavize Momoh's civil action against the government, the Home Ministry, the Immigration Department and the Prisons Department, finding that the detention was unlawful.
The court assessed general damages at RM15,000 per day for wrongful detention, while exemplary damages were fixed at RM75,000.
The court also awarded RM40,000 in costs to the plaintiff (Momoh), together with interest at the rate of five per cent per annum from the date the writ was filed on Jan 10, 2023.
On April 23, 2021, then High Court judge Datuk Ab Karim Ab Rahman ordered Momoh's immediate release after allowing his habeas corpus application.
Habeas Corpus is a writ requiring a person under arrest to be brought before a judge or to court to secure the person's release unless lawful grounds are shown for their detention.
On March 15, the same year, the 38-year-old man was arrested at a roadblock in Cheras on suspicion of drink-driving.
He pleaded guilty to the offence and was sentenced to a day's jail and fined RM12,000 by the magistrate's court.
The father of two served his term and paid the fine.
However, instead of being released, Momoh was taken to the Kajang prison where he was held until April 7 before being transferred to the Immigration Department's detention centre at Semenyih.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gaza flotilla activists return to Ireland, UK
Article:
DUBLIN: British and Irish activists who were part of a Gaza-bound flotilla seized and deported by Israel returned to London and Dublin on Saturday, following global condemnation over their treatment.
Among the Irish activists who arrived at Dublin Airport via Turkiye was Margaret Connolly, sister of Irish President Catherine Connolly.
She recounted being held with the other detained activists in "containers", where she said several people risked hypothermia.
Several supporters sporting keffiyeh scarves and Palestinian flags turned out to welcome the Irish activists, who described being "abused" and "tortured" in detention.
"We were split in groups and the next couple of days were some of the most horrific times I've ever experienced," said activist Tom Deasy.
Seven UK-based activists also landed at London's Stansted Aiport on Saturday, many still wearing Israeli prison tracksuits. They alleged that Israeli forces had shot rubber bullets at them when intercepting the convoy of vessels earlier this week.
"There was a woman in my prison cell who had a big hole in her leg as a result of one of those shootings," said Hannah Schafer, a 62-year-old from Wales.
Israel's far-right National Security Minister Itamar Ben Gvir sparked widespread condemnation and a diplomatic backlash Wednesday by posting a video showing the detained activists with their hands tied and foreheads on the ground.
Elliot Roberts, 34, who captained one of the boats, said he recalled Gvir entering the tent where they were held, alleging the guards were "playing Israeli music to hide the screams" of the detainees.
The UK said it had summoned Israel's most senior diplomat in Britain earlier this week following "the inflammatory video."
A leaked letter revealed Irish Prime Minister Micheal Martin urging the EU chief for "further action" against Israel over their treatment, including a ban on goods from Israeli settlements and the suspension "of parts if not all" of the EU's Association Agreement with Israel.
Since the start of the Gaza war, sparked by Hamas's Oct 7, 2023 attack on Israel, the territory has suffered severe shortages of food, medicine and other essential supplies, with Israel at ti--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ebola death toll tops 200 as fears grow over spread across Africa
Article:
ADDIS ABABA: Officials in the Democratic Republic of Congo updated the death toll from the Ebola outbreak to 204 late Saturday, hours after the Red Cross said three volunteers had died there and Uganda confirmed three new Ebola cases.
A health ministry statement said 204 deaths had been recorded in three provinces of the vast central African country, from 867 suspected cases. The last World Health Organization toll on Friday put the number of deaths at 177 from 750 suspected cases.
The World Health Organization has declared the outbreak of the highly contagious haemorrhagic fever an international emergency.
On Saturday, the African Union's health agency warned that more countries on the continent were at risk of being affected by the Ebola virus, in addition to the DRC and Uganda.
"We have 10 countries at risk," said Jean Kaseya, head of the Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention (Africa CDC), listing Angola, Burundi, the Central African Republic, the Republic of Congo, Ethiopia, Kenya, Rwanda, South Sudan, Tanzania and Zambia.
Kaseya said "high mobility and insecurity" in the region were helping spread the disease.
The new cases confirmed in Uganda on Saturday bring to five the total confirmed in the east African country since it was detected there and in the DRC on May 15. One person in Uganda has died.
The health ministry named the new patients as a Ugandan driver, a Ugandan health worker and a woman from the DRC. All are alive.
Ebola is a deadly viral disease that spreads through direct contact with bodily fluids. It can cause severe bleeding and organ failure.
The current epidemic centres on the conflict-wracked eastern DRC, where it was detected in Ituri province, which borders Uganda, before spreading to South Kivu.
- FIRST KNOWN VICTIMS -
The Red Cross said on Saturday that three Congolese volunteers had died in Ituri after apparently contracting Ebola there.
The three "were carrying out dead body management activities on March 27 as part of a humanitarian mission unrelated to Ebola", said the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC).
"At the time of the intervention, the community was not aware of the Ebola virus disease outbreak... They are among the first known victims."
Ebola has killed more than 15,000 people in Africa in the past half-century.
On Friday, the WHO raised the risk from Ebola in the DRC to its highest level – "very high."
It said the risk in central Africa was "high" but the global risk remained "low."
The outbreak, which experts suspect was circulating under the radar for some time, is caused by the less common Bundibugyo strain, for which there are no approved vaccines or treatments.
On Thursday, Uganda suspended public transport to the DRC after confirming its first two cases – one infection and one death – involving Congolese nationals who crossed the border.
It said the driver confirmed infected on Saturday had been at the wheel of the vehicle in which one of the ill Congolese nationals had travelled to Uganda.
The health worker was exposed to the virus when treating that Congolese patient.
The third case was a Congolese woman who had visited Uganda and tested positive for Ebola after returning to the DRC.
- 'EVERYONE'S PROBLEM' -
The eastern DRC has been plagued for three decades by conflict involving numerous armed groups.
State services in rural areas of Ituri have been largely absent for decades.
South Kivu is controlled by the Rwandan-backed armed group M23, which has never had to manage an epidemic like Ebola.
"This is everyone's problem," Congolese Health Minister Samuel Roger Kamba told a news conference in Addis Ababa alongside Kaseya.
He said the Kinshasa government needed to have "total control" of the DRC territory to stop the virus spreading.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israeli strikes rock Lebanon as Hizbollah signals unwavering Iran support
Article:
BEIRUT: Israel struck south Lebanon on Saturday despite a ceasefire as Hizbollah said its backer Teheran had pledged not to abandon the group.
Hizbollah said its chief Naim Qassem had received a message from Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, saying the latest proposal through Pakistani mediators aimed at ending the regional war emphasised "the demand to include Lebanon" in the broader ceasefire.
Lebanese authorities, however, have insisted the country's ongoing talks with Israel under US auspices must be independent from the Iran-US negotiations.
Israel's military has been pounding Lebanon despite an April 17 ceasefire in the country announced by US President Donald Trump, and which was recently extended for several weeks.
Hizbollah has also kept up attacks on Israeli targets in south Lebanon and in northern Israel, including targeting Israeli air defence platforms across the border on Saturday.
The group said Araghchi's message indicated Iran "will not give up its support" for Hizbollah.
Lebanon's state-run National News Agency (NNA) reported Israeli airstrikes on multiple south Lebanon locations, some after Israel issued evacuation warnings to around 15 villages.
Lebanon's military said one strike targeted an army barracks in the city of Nabatieh, wounding a soldier.
A statement from Israel's military said it "solely operates against the Hizbollah organisation and not against the Lebanese army", adding that the incident was under review.
- SANCTIONS -
Lebanon's military stressed this week that its soldiers were loyal to the institution after Washington announced sanctions that included an officer accused of sharing information with Hizbollah.
The sanctions came after the United States hosted three rounds of landmark direct talks between Lebanon and Israel aimed at ending the latest conflict.
Hizbollah drew Lebanon into the Middle East war on March 2 with rocket fire at Israel in retaliation for the killing of Iran's supreme leader in US-Israeli strikes.
Security talks with Israel are scheduled at the Pentagon for May 29, while a fourth round of negotiations is planned for June.
Hizbollah has vehemently rejected the direct talks.
On Saturday, Lebanon's health ministry said Israeli attacks had killed 3,123 people since March 2, decrying an overnight strike that damaged a hospital in the southern city of Tyre as "further proof of the Israeli enemy's violation of humanitarian law."
Israel had issued overnight evacuation warnings for two sites in Tyre.
An AFP correspondent saw shattered glass, ceiling panels blown out and damaged medical equipment at the Hiram hospital, near one of the locations.
The hospital's CEO, Dr Salman Aydibi, told AFP that around 40 patients were in the facility when the warning came.
- 'DOOMSDAY' -
"We took the patients to a safer location" elsewhere inside the hospital, he said, adding that no patients were harmed but some 30 staff sustained minor injuries.
He said the hospital was still operational.
Israel's army said it had targeted "Hizbollah infrastructure sites in Tyre", adding that "prior to the strike, steps were taken to mitigate harm to civilians."
Another AFP correspondent saw heavy damage at the targeted sites in the city.
"I don't know what happened, it felt like doomsday," said Wisam Baroud, who lives near one site.
"We quickly got dressed, rushed outside and started running," she said.
Israel's army also targeted a Hizbollah compound in east Lebanon overnight.
Lebanon's Jamaa Islamiya and its armed wing the Al-Fajr Forces said one of its members was killed in a strike in the east.
Under the terms of the ceasefire published by Washington, Israel reserves the right to act against "planned, imminent or ongoing attacks."
Israeli troops who invaded Lebanon are also operating inside an Israeli-announced "yellow line" running around 10 kilometres (six miles) deep along Lebanon's southern border.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump claims Iran deal largely negotiated, includes reopening Hormuz Strait
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said Saturday a deal with Iran had been "largely negotiated," with the proposal including opening the crucial Strait of Hormuz, though the agreement was "subject to finalisation."
"An Agreement has been largely negotiated, subject to finalisation between the United States of America, the Islamic Republic of Iran, and the various other Countries," Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform, listing a number of Middle East powers along with Turkiye and mediator Pakistan.
"In addition to many other elements of the Agreement, the Strait of Hormuz will be opened."
Among the Middle East countries whose leaders joined a call on Saturday to discuss the deal were Saudi Arabia, United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Egypt, Jordan and Bahrain, Trump said.
He added that he had a separate call with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, saying it "likewise, went very well."
"Final aspects and details of the Deal are currently being discussed, and will be announced shortly," Trump said.
His optimistic post came after Iranian officials said gaps remained between the parties, and that the dispute over its nuclear program would not be part of the initial talks. Teheran said it was finalising a 14-point framework for a deal.
Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei noted what he called "a trend towards rapprochement," but said "it does not necessarily mean that we and the United States will reach an agreement on the important issues."
"Our intention was first to draft a memorandum of understanding, a kind of framework agreement composed of 14 clauses," he said on state television.
Baqaei added that he hoped the details of a final agreement could be worked out "within a reasonable timeframe between 30 to 60 days" after the framework is finalised.
- 'ANOTHER ACT OF FOLLY' -
Iran's chief negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf had warned earlier that Washington would face a tough response if it resumes hostilities, after US media reports raised the prospect of new strikes and Iranian officials accused the US side of making "excessive demands."
"Our armed forces have rebuilt themselves during the ceasefire period in such a way that if Trump commits another act of folly and restarts the war, it will certainly be more crushing and bitter for the United States than on the first day of the war," Ghalibaf said.
He issued the warning after meeting in Teheran with Pakistan's army chief Field Marshal Asim Munir, a leading figure in international efforts to negotiate an end to the war, which broke out after the United States and Israel attacked the Islamic republic on Feb 28.
Weeks of negotiations, including historic face-to-face talks hosted by Islamabad, have still not produced a permanent resolution or restored full access to the Strait of Hormuz, choking vast quantities of global oil supply.
- 'NEITHER WAR NOR PEACE' -
The impasse has left ordinary Iranians in limbo.
"The state of 'neither war nor peace' is far filthier than war itself," 39-year-old Teheran resident Shahrzad told AFP.
"I'm about to start a new job, and I'm scared war might break out again – that I'll end up leaving the job like before, running off to another city out of fear," she said.
Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said in a call with UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres that Teheran was engaged despite "repeated betrayals of diplomacy and military aggression against Iran, along with contradictory positions and repeated excessive demands" by Washington.
Araghchi held a bevy of diplomatic calls, speaking with counterparts from Turkiye, Iraq, Qatar and Oman, Iran's official IRNA news agency said.
Qatari Emir Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani also spoke with Trump on Saturday, as well as with the UAE president and Saudi Arabia's Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman.
An official Qatari statement said Sheikh Tamim and the Saudi prince discussed efforts "aimed at calming the situation and promoting political solutions."
On another front in the war, Lebanese state media said Israel struck the country's south on Saturday, as fighting has not stopped despite an April 17 ceasefire.
Lebanon's military said one strike targeted a Lebanese army barracks in the south and wounded a soldier.
Israel said one of its soldiers was killed Friday near the border with Lebanon.
Iran-backed Hizbollah drew Lebanon into the war on March 2 with rocket fire at Israel after US-Israeli strikes killed Iran's supreme leader.
Hizbollah said Saturday its chief Naim Qassem had received a message from Araghchi indicating that Iran "will not give up its support" for the Lebanese group.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar takes over Pandan as adopted constituency
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim, who is also Parti Keadilan Rakyat (PKR) president, has taken over Pandan as his adopted parliamentary constituency following the departure of its former MP Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli from the party.
PKR vice-president Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said Prime Minister's senior political adviser Tengku Datuk Seri Zafrul Abdul Aziz had meanwhile been appointed as the party's monitoring officer for the constituency.
The Selangor Menteri Besar said the move was part of the party leadership's effort to reorganise the administration of the parliamentary service centre and party machinery in the constituency.
"So we have arranged for (Tengku Zafrul) to serve as the government's representative and, of course, the Prime Minister's representative in Pandan," he said during his speech at the Himpunan Setia Keadilan Pandan gathering here today.
Amirudin said Tengku Zafrul's appointment to oversee Pandan was made at Anwar's own request, as the Malaysian Investment Development Authority (MIDA) chairman wanted to play a more active role in Selangor politics.
"Tengku Zafrul has also been entrusted to monitor the Ampang parliamentary constituency. However, the leadership in Ampang consists of PKR members, so I do not want to interfere further. But if any issues arise in Ampang, Tengku Zafrul can also look into them," he said.
According to Amirudin, although the party faced challenges in managing constituencies vacated by former PKR leaders, it had not disrupted or weakened the party's services and operations.
"This is not something new. We experienced this before involving former PKR deputy president Datuk Seri Azmin Ali in Gombak and former vice-president Datuk Zuraida Kamaruddin in Ampang.
"That was how we managed the administration and, in the end, our victory margin in Ampang increased compared with the previous election," he said.
On May 17, Pandan MP Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli and Setiawangsa MP Nik Nazmi Nik Ahmad announced their resignations effective May 18 before leaving PKR on May 19.
Meanwhile, Tengku Zafrul, speaking at the same event, expressed hope for strong support from PKR leaders and grassroots members in Pandan to ensure the party's activities could be carried out effectively in the constituency.
"I need guidance and advice from all of you. I sincerely hope everyone will join me in reaching every corner of Ampang and Pandan, whether in housing areas or villages.
"I may not be the best, but InsyaAllah, I give my commitment that I will do my best to reach every home of those in need, regardless of background, religion or race," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Johor declares Monday a special holiday after JDT's Malaysia Cup triumph
Article:
JOHOR BARU: The Johor government has declared Monday (May 25) as a special public holiday in celebration of
Johor Darul Ta'zim's (JDT) Malaysia Cup 2025/26
victory last night.
Johor Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi said the declaration was made with the consent of the Regent of Johor, Tunku Mahkota Ismail.
"Upon the consent of His Royal Highness the Regent of Johor, the state government has agreed to declare Monday, May 25, 2026, as a special holiday for Johor in conjunction with JDT's Malaysia Cup 2025/26 triumph," he said in a statement today.
Earlier, the Southern Tigers secured the title after defeating Kuching City FC 2-0 in a thrilling final.
Onn Hafiz also expressed his gratitude to Tunku Mahkota Ismail for his leadership and continuous efforts in elevating Johor football to international prominence.
He described the victory as a meaningful gift for all Johoreans and JDT supporters.
"This victory is a gift to the people of Johor and the JDT faithful who have stood firmly behind the team through thick and thin, in every match, every season," he said.
He also thanked the players, coaching staff, management, and the wider JDT family for the achievement.
The latest success further cements JDT's dominance as the country's football powerhouse.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Wrong-way Bezza driver arrested; tests positive for drugs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have arrested a man in his late 30s over a viral video showing a car being driven against traffic along Jalan Hang Tuah here.
In a statement, Cheras district police chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Rosdi Daud said the suspect tested positive for amphetamine and methamphetamine following a urine test.
He is being investigated under Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 and Section 186 of the Penal Code. Police are expected to seek a remand order today.
The incident involved a silver-coloured Perodua Bezza
seen driving
against the flow of traffic, weaving through oncoming vehicles before mounting a road divider and rejoining the correct lane.
The video sparked public outrage, with netizens calling for stern action.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: King congratulates JDT on winning 6th Malaysia Cup title
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, has extended his congratulations to the Johor Darul Ta'zim Football Club (JDT) on their success in capturing their sixth Malaysia Cup title today.
According to a post on the Sultan Ibrahim Sultan Iskandar Facebook page, His Majesty also expressed his appreciation for the fighting spirit shown by the players and coaching staff, as well as the unwavering support of the loyal JDT fans who stand by the team through thick and thin.
"The fans have always been a source of inspiration and strength for the team, igniting their fighting spirit and providing the motivation to continue charting success," His Majesty said.
Sultan Ibrahim also extended his congratulations to his son, Tunku Mahkota Ismail, the Regent of Johor, who is also the owner of JDT.
JDT created history tonight by becoming the first team to win the Malaysia Cup for four consecutive seasons after defeating Kuching City FC 2-0 in the 2025/2026 Malaysia Cup final at the National Stadium in Bukit Jalil here.
The
Southern Tigers
' two goals were scored in the second half by Marcos Guilherme and substitute Arif Aiman Mohd Hanapi.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bangladesh's rare 'Donald Trump' buffalo becomes Eid sensation
Article:
DHAKA: A rare albino buffalo with flowing blond hair has become an unlikely celebrity in Bangladesh ahead of the Eid al-Adha festival, drawing crowds of curious visitors who say the animal bears a striking resemblance to US President Donald Trump.
The nearly 700 kg (1,540 lb) buffalo, raised at a farm in Narayanganj district near the capital Dhaka, has been nicknamed "Donald Trump" because of the tuft of pale hair falling across its forehead — a feature many say mirrors the American leader's trademark hairstyle.
Dozens of people flocked to the farm daily, with many travelling from distant districts to pose for selfies and videos with the unusually coloured animal, which quickly went viral on social media.
"My younger brother named the buffalo Donald Trump because the hair on the front of its head resembles Donald Trump," farm owner Ziauddin Mridha said.
"Despite its striking appearance, it is very calm by nature. It is an albino buffalo, and animals of this type are generally gentle and do not become aggressive unless provoked," he added.
[[nid:1446719]]
Unusual animals go viral during Eid festival
Mridha said the buffalo required special care, including being bathed four times a day and fed four meals daily to keep it healthy and in good condition before Eid.
"It needed extra attention and care every day. The buffalo has already been delivered to a customer for sacrifice during Eid," he said.
Albino buffaloes are considered rare in Bangladesh, where most buffaloes are dark-skinned. The animal's cream-coloured body, pinkish nose and long blond hair made it stand out among thousands of cattle prepared for the Muslim festival of Eid al-Adha, when families traditionally sacrifice livestock.
In recent years, the Muslim-majority country has seen unusually large or distinctive sacrificial animals become viral Eid attractions, often given celebrity-inspired names to draw buyers and visitors.
The buffalo's resemblance to Trump, combined with its size and gentle temperament, has made it one of this year's biggest crowd-pullers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran, US finalising memorandum of understanding - Official
Article:
TEHERAN: Iran and Washington are at the final stage of work on a 14-point memorandum of understanding, Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Ismail Baghaei said on Saturday, reported Sputnik/RIA Novosti.
"Our goal was to develop a 14-point memorandum of understanding, which would cover the most important issues necessary to end the conflict and those of fundamental importance to us. We are currently finalising this memorandum of understanding," Baghaei was quoted as saying by Iran's
Fars news agency
.
The key points of the memorandum relate to the issues of cessation of hostilities between Iran and the United States, the lifting of the maritime blockade placed on Iran and the unfreezing of Iran's sovereign assets held in foreign banks, the spokesman added.
Meanwhile, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio on Saturday announced progress on Iran and suggested there could be news soon.
"I don't have news for you at this very moment, but there might be some news a little later today. There may not be. I hope there will be ... As I said, there's been some progress done," Rubio told reporters in New York.
– Bernama-Sputnik/RIA Novosti
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: JDT star Arif Aiman to end bachelorhood next week
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Johor Darul Ta'zim (JDT) sensation Arif Aiman Hanapi is confirmed to be ending his bachelorhood this Monday.
The news was confirmed following the circulation of the national winger's wedding invitation, which has been widely shared across social media platforms.
According to the invitation, the 23-year-old will be united with his partner, Nurul Aliah Natasha Md Hairushahri, in a ceremony scheduled to take place in Johor Baru.
The wedding is set to be a significant event for the local football fraternity.
"The ceremony is expected to take place in a lively atmosphere with the presence of family members, close acquaintances, and several important figures in Malaysian football," Harian Metro reported.
Among the high-profile guests expected to grace the occasion is the Regent of Johor, Tunku Ismail Sultan Ibrahim, who is also the owner of JDT.
Arif Aiman is currently regarded as one of the most vital assets for both his club and the national team, Harimau Malaya.
His rise to stardom has been marked by a series of consistent and stellar performances over recent seasons.
The agile winger is considered among the best prospects in Malaysian football today following his excellent performances with the Southern Tigers on both the local and Asian stages.
In addition to his individual accolades, Arif Aiman has been instrumental in helping JDT maintain its dominance in the domestic football scene.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Queen arrives in Turkiye for special visit
Article:
ISTANBUL: Her Majesty Raja Zarith Sofiah, Queen of Malaysia, has arrived in Istanbul, Turkiye in conjunction with a three-day special visit to the country.
Her Majesty was accompanied by the Tunku Temenggong of Johor Tunku Idris Iskandar Al-Haj Ibni Sultan Ibrahim, Tunku Panglima of Johor Tunku Abdul Rahman Al-Haj Ibni Sultan Ibrahim and Tunku Putera of Johor Tunku Abu Bakar Al-Haj Ibni Sultan Ibrahim.
The private aircraft carrying the royal entourage arrived at Istanbul Airport at 6.10pm local time (11.10pm Malaysian time) and was received by Turkiye's Deputy Director of Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Mehmet Zahid Uzun and Deputy Governor of Istanbul, Mustafa Kaya.
Also present were Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin, Malaysia's Consul General in Istanbul Ahmad Amiri Abu Bakar, Istana Negara Grand Chamberlain Datuk Azuan Effendy Zairakithnaini and Comptroller of the Royal Household of Johor Colonel (Rtd) Datuk Mohamed Perang Musa.
During the visit, Raja Zarith Sofiah is scheduled to attend the Naming and Launching Ceremony of a Royal Malaysian Navy ship tomorrow at the Istanbul Shipyard.
The Queen departed for Turkiye from the Royal Hangar at Senai International Airport, Johor, this morning.
– Bernama
TAGS: Raja Zarith Sofiah, Her Majesty, special visit, Turkiye, Royal Malaysian Navy, ship
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Teenage girl reported missing while hiking in Tanjong Malim, search underway
Article:
IPOH, May 24 — A teenage girl has been reported missing after possibly being lost while hiking at Bukit Changkat Asa near Tanjong Malim yesterday.
Perak Fire and Rescue Department operations division assistant director Sabarodzi Nor Ahmad said they received a call at 1.26 pm and dispatched a team of firefighters to the location.
“A girl, Nur Izzati Humaira Azizul, 19, is believed to have gotten lost while descending the hill during a hike with five friends and family members,” he said in a statement last night, adding that the search began at the last place she was seen.
The search and rescue operation was suspended at 7 pm yesterday and would be handed over to the Kuala Kubu Bharu Fire and Rescue Department for further action, he added. — Bernama

Title: MB: Johor declares Monday special holiday after JDT win Malaysia Cup
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, May 24 — The Johor state government has declared a special state holiday on Monday following Johor Darul Ta’zim’s (JDT) Malaysia Cup win last night.
The Southern Tigers beat Kuching City FC 2-0 in the final at National Stadium, Bukit Jalil tonight to win their fourth consecutive Malaysia Cup title, the only club to have ever achieved the feat.
Johor Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi said the declaration was made with the consent of Johor Regent, Tunku Mahkota Ismail in appreciation to the Johor people and Southern Tiger fans for their undivided support to the team.
“The win is a gift for Bangsa Johor and the fans who have stood united behind the team through thick and thin, in every match and season,” he posted on Facebook last night, as he also congratulated the players, coaches and team management and the JDT farternity.
The success proved the continued commitment of the Johor leadership in developing modern high performance football that made JDT the state’s pride and a respected team at the national and Asian level, he added. — Bernama

Title: King congratulates JDT on historic fourth straight Malaysia Cup win
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 24 — His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, has extended his congratulations to the Johor Darul Ta’zim Football Club (JDT) on their success in capturing their sixth Malaysia Cup title yesterday.
According to a post on the Sultan Ibrahim Sultan Iskandar Facebook page, His Majesty also expressed his appreciation for the fighting spirit shown by the players and coaching staff, as well as the unwavering support of the loyal JDT fans who stand by the team through thick and thin.
“The fans have always been a source of inspiration and strength for the team, igniting their fighting spirit and providing the motivation to continue charting success,” His Majesty said.
Sultan Ibrahim also extended his congratulations to his son, Tunku Mahkota Ismail, the Regent of Johor, who is also the owner of JDT.
JDT created history tonight by becoming the first team to win the Malaysia Cup for four consecutive seasons after defeating Kuching City FC 2-0 in the 2025/2026 Malaysia Cup final at the National Stadium in Bukit Jalil here.
The Southern Tigers’ two goals were scored in the second half by Marcos Guilherme and substitute Arif Aiman Mohd Hanapi. — Bernama

Title: Anwar takes over Pandan as adopted constituency after Rafizi’s exit, Tengku Zafrul named monitoring officer
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 24 — Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim, who is also Parti Keadilan Rakyat (PKR) president, has taken over Pandan as his adopted parliamentary constituency following the departure of its former MP Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli from the party.
PKR vice-president Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said Prime Minister’s senior political adviser Datuk Seri Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz had meanwhile been appointed as the party’s monitoring officer for the constituency.
The Selangor Menteri Besar said the move was part of the party leadership’s effort to reorganise the administration of the parliamentary service centre and party machinery in the constituency.
“So we have arranged for (Tengku Zafrul) to serve as the government’s representative and, of course, the Prime Minister’s representative in Pandan,” he said during his speech at the Himpunan Setia Keadilan Pandan gathering here yesterday.
Amirudin said Tengku Zafrul’s appointment to oversee Pandan was made at Anwar’s own request, as the Malaysian Investment Development Authority (MIDA) chairman wanted to play a more active role in Selangor politics.
“Tengku Zafrul has also been entrusted to monitor the Ampang parliamentary constituency. However, the leadership in Ampang consists of PKR members, so I do not want to interfere further. But if any issues arise in Ampang, Tengku Zafrul can also look into them,” he said.
According to Amirudin, although the party faced challenges in managing constituencies vacated by former PKR leaders, it had not disrupted or weakened the party’s services and operations.
“This is not something new. We experienced this before involving former PKR deputy president Datuk Seri Azmin Ali in Gombak and former vice-president Datuk Zuraida Kamaruddin in Ampang.
“That was how we managed the administration and, in the end, our victory margin in Ampang increased compared with the previous election,” he said.
On May 17, Pandan MP Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli and Setiawangsa MP Nik Nazmi Nik Ahmad announced their resignations effective May 18 before leaving PKR on May 19.
Meanwhile, Tengku Zafrul, speaking at the same event, expressed hope for strong support from PKR leaders and grassroots members in Pandan to ensure the party’s activities could be carried out effectively in the constituency.
“I need guidance and advice from all of you. I sincerely hope everyone will join me in reaching every corner of Ampang and Pandan, whether in housing areas or villages.
“I may not be the best, but InsyaAllah, I give my commitment that I will do my best to reach every home of those in need, regardless of background, religion or race,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Police declare bomb threat at prominent KL building a hoax after sweep, probe underway
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 24 — Police have confirmed that a bomb threat made against a prominent building in the capital last night was false.
Brickfields district police chief ACP Hoo Chang Hook said police received a call regarding the incident at 9.09 pm from a security guard at the building.
“The building’s security guard informed us that he had received an email sent to the customer service centre stating that an individual had placed a bomb on the 23rd floor of the building’s north tower,” he said in a statement.
According to him, a Bomb Disposal Unit, K9 Unit and Forensic Unit were then deployed to the location to conduct a search and found that it was a false threat.
The case is being investigated under Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 and Section 507 of the Penal Code. — Bernama

Title: One shock away from crisis: Report says nearly a fifth of PPR households have no savings, over half can last only three months or less
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 24 — Public housing in Malaysia, originally designed as short-term shelter for the urban poor, now risks keeping its tenants for far longer than intended, according to a recent Think City study.
The report found that almost three-quarters of some 3,000 People’s Housing Project (PPR) residents surveyed are vulnerable to economic shocks.
Nearly 20 per cent of households surveyed said they had no savings at all, while 56 per cent said they had enough savings to last only one to three months.
The report, titled “From Roof to Resilience”, polled 2,884 people across 10 public housing communities in the Klang Valley. It was part of Think City’s Kita-untuk-Kita (K2K) initiative, funded by the Finance Ministry.
Around 40 per cent of the households rely on a single income-earner and close to one in 10 households — mostly with at least one person with disabilities (PWD) — reported having no breadwinner at all.
The situation has led to multigenerational households, where grandparents, parents and children live under the same roof, with most respondents saying they have lived in public housing for over 30 years.
Nearly half of the households surveyed also had more than five family members — higher than the national average of about four people in a house.
“It also suggests that the next generation who have grown up in public housing are unable to achieve social mobility and remain stuck where they are.
“Of concern is the fact that eight out of the 10 sites had multigenerational households of more than 5 per cent, suggesting that one in 20 households were stuck in a poverty trap in these geographies,” the report said.
To make matters worse, living conditions for most PPR residents surveyed appeared to have either stagnated or further declined since the Covid-19 pandemic.
Enduring pervasive littering, vandalised infrastructure and recurring lift breakdowns have become a daily routine for most of them.
Nearly half of the 3,000 respondents said they had never used the public facilities in their PPRs due to hygiene reasons and safety concerns because of rubbish thrown from the upper floors.
Lift breakdowns, which some residents said have taken years to resolve, have also forced some residents to pay more for household necessities.
“In PPR Bandar Tun Razak, for instance, residents reported that gas cylinder cost rises from RM28 to RM50 every time the lift stops working.
“Residents also report paying higher costs for bottled water and other services like food and grocery delivery due to the frequent lift breakdowns,” the report noted.
